title:
3989. Partition Array into K Subsets with Equal Sum
Coding and Problem-SolvingData Structures & Algorithms (DSA)Dynamic

transcript.text:
3989. Partition Array into K Subsets with Equal Sum
Coding and Problem-SolvingData Structures & Algorithms (DSA)Dynamic Programming (DP)BacktrackingArraysCombinatorics
Hard
Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return true if it is possible to divide this array into k non-empty subsets whose sums are all equal. Otherwise, return false.

Input Format:
nums: An array of integers. k: An integer representing the number of subsets.

Output Format:
true if possible, false otherwise.

Example 1:
Input:
nums = [4, 3, 2, 3, 5, 2, 1], k = 4
Output: true
Explanation:
It is possible to partition the array into 4 subsets each summing to 5:
(5), (4,1), (3,2), (3,2)
Constraints:
1 <= k <= len(nums) <= 16
0 <= nums[i] <= 10000
The sum of nums is divisible by k.

blocks.md:
## 1. Problem Understanding + Constraints Analysis We're given an array `nums` and an integer `k`. We must decide: can we split ALL elements into exactly `k` groups (every element used exactly once, no group empty) such that every group has the SAME sum? Since every element must be used and all `k` groups sum to the same value, that value is forced: **target = total_sum / k**. The problem even tells us the sum is divisible by `k`, so target is always a whole number. > 💬 "So the task is to partition the whole array into k buckets where each bucket has identical sum. Because all elements are consumed and the sums are equal, each bucket must sum to total/k. My job is to figure out if such a packing exists." **Constraint Analysis:** | Constraint | Value | Implication | |---|---|---| | n = len(nums) | ≤ 16 | Tiny! This SCREAMS bitmask / exponential. 2^16 = 65,536 states is trivial. O(n²·2ⁿ) ≈ 16²·65536 ≈ 17M — fine | | k | 1 ≤ k ≤ n | k=1 → trivially true; k=n → every element must equal target | | nums[i] | 0 to 10000 | Non-negative, and **zeros are allowed** — a subtle edge case | | sum % k | == 0 (guaranteed) | target = sum/k is an integer; still must check no single element > target | The `n ≤ 16` is the loudest signal in the whole problem. Whenever you see n ≤ ~20, the intended solution is almost always **exponential in n** — either backtracking or bitmask DP over 2ⁿ subsets. **Clarifying questions to ask:** - "Can `nums` contain zeros or negative numbers?" (Constraints say 0 to 10000 — non-negative, but zeros exist.) - "Is the array guaranteed non-empty?" (1 ≤ n.) - "Is it guaranteed the sum is divisible by k, or should I check that myself?" (Given as guaranteed, but I'll still guard it.) - "Which language do you prefer — I'll write Python." > 💬 "Quick clarifications: values are non-negative but can include zero, right? And the sum is guaranteed divisible by k — but I'll still defensively check it and check that no single element exceeds the target, since one oversized element makes it impossible." --- ## 2. Understand It On Paper Take Example 1: `nums = [4, 3, 2, 3, 5, 2, 1]`, `k = 4`. ``` sum = 4+3+2+3+5+2+1 = 20 target per subset = 20 / 4 = 5 ``` So I need to carve the array into 4 buckets, each summing to exactly 5: ``` bucket 1: (5) -> 5 ✓ bucket 2: (4, 1) -> 5 ✓ bucket 3: (3, 2) -> 5 ✓ bucket 4: (3, 2) -> 5 ✓ all 7 elements used, 4 buckets, each = 5 => TRUE ``` **The key insight, visually:** think of it as filling `k` empty boxes, each with capacity `target`. We drop elements into boxes one at a time; a box may never overflow past `target`. If we can place every element with all boxes ending exactly full, the answer is true. ``` target = 5 ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ ┌─────┐ box: │ 5 │ │ 4+1 │ │ 3+2 │ │ 3+2 │ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ └─────┘ 5 5 5 5 ``` **Why naive trying is wasteful:** if we try "for each element, which box does it go in?" by raw recursion, we explore k^n placements (4^7 = 16,384 here, but blows up fast). Tons of those branches are symmetric — putting the first element in empty box #1 vs empty box #2 is the SAME thing. That symmetry is the redundancy we'll kill. **PREREQUISITE — bitmask as a "used set":** Since n ≤ 16, we can represent *which elements are already used* as a single integer where bit `i` = 1 means `nums[i]` is taken. Example with 4 elements: ``` mask = 1011 (binary) = elements 0, 1, 3 are used; element 2 is free ↑↑↑↑ bit: 3210 check if element 2 is used: mask & (1 << 2) -> 1011 & 0100 = 0000 -> free mark element 2 as used: mask | (1 << 2) -> 1011 | 0100 = 1111 ``` This lets us memoize on "set of used elements" — there are only 2ⁿ such sets, so we never recompute the same subset twice. 🗣️ **Hinglish:** "Dekh, socho 4 dabbe hain, har dabbe ki capacity 5 hai. Elements ek-ek karke daalte jao, koi dabba overflow nahi hona chahiye. Agar saare elements fit ho gaye aur har dabba exactly full ho gaya — toh answer haan hai. Aur kaunse elements use ho chuke hain woh ek binary number se yaad rakhte hain, taaki same combination dobara na try karein." --- ## 3. Approach & Intuition **Pattern recognition:** This is the classic "**K equal-sum subsets**" problem (LeetCode 698). The tells: - Small n (≤ 16) → exponential search is intended. - "Partition into groups with a property" → **backtracking** (try placements, undo on failure). - "Avoid recomputing the same subset of used elements" → **bitmask DP / memoization** over 2ⁿ states. **The core intuition:** Instead of asking "which box does each element go in" (lots of symmetric duplication), we fill **one box at a time**. We greedily complete bucket after bucket: keep adding elements until the current bucket hits `target`, then "reset" and start the next bucket. We only ever track *which elements are used* (a bitmask) and *how full the current bucket is*. When all elements are used and the current fill is back to 0, we've successfully closed exactly k buckets. > 💬 "I'll fill one subset at a time rather than assigning each element to one of k boxes — that removes a lot of symmetric duplicate work. I track used elements as a bitmask, which lets me memoize and never re-explore the same set twice. With n up to 16, 2^16 states is totally affordable." The reason "fill one bucket at a time" works: if the total can be split into k equal buckets, then in particular *some* subset sums to target; remove it, and the rest must split into k−1 equal buckets — same problem, smaller. So completing buckets sequentially loses no generality. --- ## 4. Brute Force → Why It Fails **1. The brute force approach:** For each of the n elements, decide which of the k buckets it goes into. Recurse over all assignments, then at the end check all bucket sums equal target. That's **k^n** combinations. **2. Why it fails on these constraints:** With n = 16 and k = 16, k^n = 16^16 ≈ 1.8 × 10^19 — astronomically beyond any time limit (10^9 ops/sec would take ~580 years). Even moderate k explodes. Pure unpruned assignment is hopeless. **3. The redundant work:** Two big sources of waste: - **Symmetry:** placing an element into empty bucket A vs empty bucket B yields identical future states, yet brute force treats them as different branches — multiplying work by up to k!. - **Repeated subsets:** different assignment orders reach the SAME set of "used elements with the same current-bucket fill," and we re-solve that identical subproblem over and over. **4. The key insight that fixes it:** - Fill **one bucket at a time** (kills the bucket-symmetry factor). - Memoize on the **bitmask of used elements** — there are only 2ⁿ such sets, so each is solved once. Combined with pruning (skip duplicates, skip elements that overflow the bucket), the search collapses from k^n to roughly O(n · 2ⁿ). > 💬 "Let me start with the brute force to show my reasoning: assign each element to one of k buckets — that's k^n, which for n=16 is around 10^19 and instantly TLEs. The waste is symmetry between empty buckets and re-solving identical used-element sets. I'll fix both: fill one bucket at a time, and memoize on a bitmask of used elements. That brings it down to about n times 2^n, which is fine for n ≤ 16." --- ## 5. Optimal Approach **1. The core idea in one sentence:** Fill buckets one at a time using backtracking, tracking used elements as a bitmask and memoizing failed states, completing each bucket to exactly `target` before starting the next. **2. Why it works:** If a valid k-partition exists, some subset sums to `target`; peeling it off leaves a valid (k−1)-partition of the rest. So sequentially closing buckets explores the full solution space, and the bitmask lets us treat "set of used elements" as a state we solve at most once. **3. The steps:** 1. Compute `total`. If not divisible by k (defensive) → false. `target = total / k`. 2. If any single element > target → false (can never fit). 3. **Sort descending** — big elements first prunes dead branches fast. 4. Recurse: track `used` bitmask and `current_sum` of the bucket being filled. 5. When `current_sum == target`, that bucket is closed; start a fresh bucket (reset sum to 0) and continue. 6. When all elements are used → true. Memoize masks that lead to failure. **4. Trace on a tiny example:** `nums = [3, 3, 2, 2]`, `k = 2`. total = 10, target = 5. Sort desc → `[3, 3, 2, 2]`. We fill buckets to 5. Track `(used mask, current_sum)`. mask bits index the sorted array `[3,3,2,2]` = bits 0,1,2,3. ``` Start: mask=0000, current_sum=0, buckets_done=0 ``` Step 1 — start bucket 1, add nums[0]=3: ``` mask=0001, current_sum=3 (3 < 5, keep filling) ``` Step 2 — try nums[1]=3: 3+3=6 > 5 → overflow, skip. Try nums[2]=2: 3+2=5 = target ✓ ``` mask=0101, current_sum=5 -> bucket 1 CLOSED (used: nums[0]=3, nums[2]=2) reset: current_sum=0, buckets_done=1, start bucket 2 ``` Step 3 — start bucket 2 from remaining {nums[1]=3, nums[3]=2}. Add nums[1]=3: ``` mask=0111, current_sum=3 (3 < 5) ``` Step 4 — add nums[3]=2: 3+2=5 = target ✓ ``` mask=1111, current_sum=5 -> bucket 2 CLOSED all elements used => TRUE ``` ``` Final partition: bucket1 = (3,2)=5 , bucket2 = (3,2)=5 ✓ ``` > 💬 "I fill bucket one to 5 using the 3 and a 2, close it, then fill bucket two with the other 3 and 2. All elements consumed, both buckets equal 5 — return true. If at any point no element fits the current bucket, I backtrack; and I remember failed used-sets so I don't retry them." 🗣️ **Hinglish:** "Ek dabba pura bharo target tak, band karo, agla shuru karo. Jo element fit nahi hota use chhod do; jo used-set fail ho chuka hai use bitmask mein yaad rakho — dobara mat try karo." --- ## 6. Solution (interview-style code — NO execution) ```python def can_partition_k_subsets(nums, k): # ---- Edge cases & guards (handled up front, not buried) ---- n = len(nums) if k <= 0: return False if k == 1: return True # whole array is the single subset if k > n: return False # can't make more non-empty subsets than elements total = sum(nums) if total % k != 0: # defensive: problem guarantees divisibility, but guard anyway return False target = total // k # If any single element exceeds the target it can never fit in a bucket. # (Sorting descending also lets the largest, hardest-to-place elements fail fast.) nums.sort(reverse=True) if nums[0] > target: return False full_mask = (1 << n) - 1 # all bits set => every element used # memo[mask] = True means: starting from this set of used elements with a # FRESH (empty) current bucket, we already know it CANNOT be completed. # We only memoize the "current_sum reset to 0" boundary, so the state is # fully described by `mask` alone (current_sum is implied = total_used % target). failed = set() def backtrack(used_mask, current_sum): # All elements placed -> every bucket got filled exactly. Success. if used_mask == full_mask: return True # When a bucket just got completed, current_sum == 0 here, and `used_mask` # alone is a memoizable boundary state. if current_sum == 0 and used_mask in failed: return False for i in range(n): # Skip already-used elements. if used_mask & (1 << i): continue # Pruning: if this element overflows the current bucket, skip it. # Because nums is sorted descending, once one element is too big, # we still must check the rest (they're smaller), so we 'continue'. if current_sum + nums[i] > target: continue next_sum = current_sum + nums[i] new_mask = used_mask | (1 << i) # If bucket is now exactly full, close it and start a fresh one (sum -> 0). # Otherwise keep filling the same bucket. if backtrack(new_mask, next_sum % target): return True # ⚠️ Optimization & correctness note: # If current_sum == 0, we just tried placing nums[i] as the FIRST element # of a brand-new bucket and it failed. Any other element we pick as the # first of this bucket is symmetric, so we can stop trying — break. # This is the key pruning that makes it fast. if current_sum == 0: break # Record this fresh-bucket boundary as a dead end. if current_sum == 0: failed.add(used_mask) return False return backtrack(0, 0) ``` ⚠️ **Self-flagged points:** - `next_sum % target`: when `next_sum == target` the bucket is full, `% target` resets it to 0 → automatically "starts a new bucket." Since `target ≥ 1` here (if target were 0 we'd have all-zero arrays — see edge cases) this is safe. **I must guard target == 0 separately** (covered in §9). - The `break` when `current_sum == 0` after a failed first-placement is the symmetry-breaking prune — without it the search is far slower but still correct. - Memoizing only at the `current_sum == 0` boundary keeps the state = `mask` alone, avoiding a `(mask, sum)` blowup. **Mental dry run on Example 1** (`[4,3,2,3,5,2,1]`, k=4, target=5, sorted desc `[5,4,3,3,2,2,1]`): - Start bucket: pick 5 → sum hits 5 → bucket closed, mask has bit for 5. - New bucket: pick 4 → sum=4; need 1 → pick the 1 → sum=5 → closed. - New bucket: pick 3 → sum=3; pick a 2 → sum=5 → closed. - New bucket: pick remaining 3 → sum=3; pick remaining 2 → sum=5 → closed. - All 7 elements used → `used_mask == full_mask` → **return True.** ✓ --- ## 7. Code Walkthrough Walking through with `nums = [3,3,2,2]`, `k=2` (sorted desc `[3,3,2,2]`, target=5, full_mask = 1111): 1. `backtrack(0000, 0)` — fresh bucket. Loop i=0: `nums[0]=3`, 0+3=3 ≤ 5. Recurse `backtrack(0001, 3)`. 2. `backtrack(0001, 3)` — current_sum=3 (not a boundary). i=1: 3+3=6 > 5 skip. i=2: `nums[2]=2`, 3+2=5 ≤ 5. `next_sum = 5 % 5 = 0`. Recurse `backtrack(0101, 0)`. 3. `backtrack(0101, 0)` — fresh bucket (bucket 1 just closed). i=1: `nums[1]=3`, 0+3=3 ≤ 5. Recurse `backtrack(0111, 3)`. 4. `backtrack(0111, 3)` — i=3: `nums[3]=2`, 3+2=5. `next_sum = 0`. Recurse `backtrack(1111, 0)`. 5. `backtrack(1111, 0)` — `used_mask == full_mask` → **return True**. The `True` propagates all the way up. ✓ ```mermaid flowchart TD A["mask=0000 sum=0
(start bucket 1)"] --> B["mask=0001 sum=3
took nums0=3"] B --> C["mask=0101 sum=0
took nums2=2, bucket1 full → reset"] C --> D["mask=0111 sum=3
took nums1=3 (start bucket 2)"] D --> E["mask=1111 sum=0
took nums3=2, bucket2 full"] E --> F["used==full_mask → TRUE"] ``` The `failed` set only gets populated when a fresh-bucket state dead-ends — here we hit success on the first path, so nothing was memoized. On harder inputs (lots of false answers), that memo + the `break` pruning is what keeps us fast. --- ## 8. Complexity Analysis **Time: O(n · 2ⁿ).** - There are 2ⁿ possible `used_mask` values. Each fresh-bucket boundary state (`current_sum == 0`) is solved at most once thanks to the `failed` memo. From each state we do an O(n) loop over candidate elements. So the dominant cost is O(n · 2ⁿ) ≈ 16 · 65,536 ≈ 1.05M core operations — easily within limits. - The `break`-on-empty-bucket symmetry prune and the descending sort cut the *practical* runtime far below the worst case, but O(n · 2ⁿ) is the bound to state. **Space: O(2ⁿ).** - The `failed` set can hold up to 2ⁿ masks. The recursion depth is at most n (we add one element per recursive level before a bucket closes), so the call stack is O(n). Total dominated by the memo: **O(2ⁿ)**. **Contrast with brute force:** brute force assignment is O(k^n) (≈ 10^19 for n=k=16). We dropped to O(n · 2ⁿ) ≈ 10^6 — about 13 orders of magnitude better — purely by filling one bucket at a time and memoizing on the used-set. This is *not* in-place; we use O(2ⁿ) auxiliary memory for the memo. --- ## 9. Edge Cases & Pitfalls | Edge case | What breaks naively | How this code handles it | |---|---|---| | `k == 1` | Some impls loop needlessly | Early return `True` | | `k > n` | Can't form k non-empty subsets | Early return `False` | | `sum % k != 0` | target not integer → wrong logic | Guard returns `False` (even though guaranteed) | | One element > target | Infinite/failed search | `if nums[0] > target: return False` after desc sort | | Zeros in array | A zero "fits" any bucket → can inflate empty-bucket placements; subtle but the logic still completes correctly since zeros add 0 and get consumed | Loop still places them; correctness preserved. ⚠️ but see target==0 below | | **All zeros** (`target == 0`) | `next_sum % target` → **division by zero!** | ⚠️ Must special-case: if `target == 0`, every element is 0 and any partition into k non-empty groups works → return `True` (since k ≤ n guarantees we can make k non-empty groups). **Add this guard before recursion.** | | Single element, k=1 | fine | handled by `k==1` | | Largest element == target | Should immediately close a bucket | Works: `nums[i] == target` → `next_sum % target == 0` | ⚠️ **The most important fix to mention:** the `target == 0` (all-zeros) case. Add right after computing target: ```python if target == 0: return True # all elements are 0 and k <= n, so k non-empty zero-sum groups exist ``` Without it, `next_sum % target` divides by zero. An interviewer will probe this — call it out proactively. **Common interview traps for this problem type:** - Forgetting the **symmetry break** (`break` on empty bucket) — code is correct but TLEs on adversarial inputs. - Memoizing on `(mask, current_sum)` instead of just `mask` at boundaries — wastes memory; `current_sum` is derivable. - Not sorting descending — drastically slower pruning. - Assuming sum-divisibility without checking (don't rely on the guarantee blindly). > 💬 **30-second wrap-up:** "Since n is at most 16, this is a bitmask backtracking problem. Each subset must sum to total/k. I fill one bucket at a time, tracking used elements as a bitmask and memoizing failed used-sets, which collapses the naive k^n assignment search down to about n·2ⁿ — around a million operations. I sort descending and break on symmetric empty-bucket placements to prune hard. Key edge cases: any element bigger than target is an instant false, and the all-zeros case where target is 0 must return true while avoiding a divide-by-zero. Overall O(n·2ⁿ) time, O(2ⁿ) space."
